notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a clean SEA–SFO + Microsoft-focused checklist. Treat each line as: “explicitly confirm this; don’t assume it.”

1. Airline status & basic benefits (Alaska)

Explicitly confirm:

Your Alaska Mileage Plan status (None / MVP / MVP Gold / 75K / 100K) and:
Free checked bags allowance
Priority boarding group
Complimentary upgrades eligibility (Main → Premium / First, and when they clear)
Whether your fare class (Saver vs Main vs First) is:
Eligible for upgrades
Eligible for same-day changes
Earning full miles/elite credit

Don’t assume:

Saver fares include free changes, upgrades, or seat selection.
Status guarantees an upgrade; it’s always space available and priority-based.
2. Credit card perks (Alaska or general travel cards)

Explicitly confirm:

If your card is allowed for this trip (Microsoft often requires a corporate card for reimbursable expenses).
Card benefits that apply only if you pay with that card, like:
Free checked bag on Alaska (often for you + companions on the same reservation)
Priority boarding
Trip delay/cancellation/interruption insurance
Rental car primary/secondary coverage

Don’t assume:

You’ll be reimbursed for using a personal card if policy says use corporate.
Card insurance applies when the company paid or a different card was used.
3. Microsoft travel policy & booking rules (high level, public-style)

Explicitly confirm (via MS internal resources / travel portal):

Required booking channel (e.g., Concur / designated TMC) and whether:
You can book directly with Alaska and still be reimbursed.
You can use personal loyalty numbers and still comply.
Class of service allowed SEA–SFO (usually economy; premium/First often needs justification/approval).
What’s reimbursable vs not, including:
Seat selection fees (extra legroom, Premium, exit row)
Checked bags
Lounge day passes
In-flight Wi‑Fi and meals
Change/cancellation rules:
When you must rebook through the travel tool vs directly with the airline
Whether same-day change fees are reimbursable

Don’t assume:

Premium seats, lounge access, or early boarding are reimbursable just because it’s a work trip.
You can keep credits/vouchers personally if the company paid for the ticket. Check policy.
4. Lounge access (SEA & SFO)

Explicitly confirm:

Whether you have any of these:
Alaska Lounge membership
Credit card with Priority Pass or Alaska Lounge access
Status-based access (usually only with First or certain international itineraries)
Which specific lounges your access covers at SEA and SFO, and guest rules.
If day passes are reimbursable (often they’re not).

Don’t assume:

Domestic economy or status alone gets you into lounges.
Priority Pass works at every lounge in SEA/SFO (locations and hours vary).
5. Seat upgrades & extra legroom

Explicitly confirm:

If your fare type allows:
Paid upgrades
Complimentary elite upgrades
Paid Premium Class / exit row
Whether paid seat fees are:
Personally paid (for comfort)
Reimbursable under Microsoft policy

Don’t assume:

Work travel justifies First Class or Premium without prior approval.
You’ll be reimbursed for seat fees you chose for comfort.
6. Baggage & same-day changes

Explicitly confirm:

Your free bag allowance from:
Alaska status
Credit card
Fare type
Same-day change/standby rules:
Fee amount
Whether your fare is eligible
If Microsoft will reimburse change fees for voluntary vs business-need changes

Don’t assume:

Your carry-on will always fit. SEA–SFO can have full overheads and gate-checks.
Same-day changes are free or reimbursable if it’s just for your convenience.
7. Trip protections & delays

Explicitly confirm:

Whether coverage comes from:
Corporate travel insurance
Your credit card (only if used to pay)
Airline policies (e.g., meal vouchers for long delays)
What’s reimbursable for:
Hotel if stranded
Meals during long delays
Ground transport if diverted

Don’t assume:

The airline will cover hotels/meals for weather or ATC delays.
Microsoft will reimburse any expense during a delay without receipts or policy alignment.
8. Reimbursement mechanics

Explicitly confirm:

Required receipts (thresholds, itemization).
Per diem vs actuals for meals.
Deadlines for expense submission.
Whether tips, Wi‑Fi, and in-flight purchases are reimbursable.

Don’t assume:

Small charges “don’t matter” and can be expensed without documentation.
You can mix personal and business travel on one ticket without checking how to split costs.

If you’d like, you can paste your exact SEA–SFO itinerary details (fare type, status level, and what card you plan to use), and I can turn this into a one-page, trip-specific do/don’t list.
